title: Obamacare rolls shrank dramatically in many states over the past year, new federal data shows
summary: Federal data reveals approximately 2.6 million fewer Americans had ACA marketplace coverage in February 2026 compared to the prior year, marking the first complete 50-state breakdown following the January expiration of enhanced subsidy provisions. Ohio and Oklahoma suffered the steepest losses, each shedding nearly one-third of their enrollees. The CMS dataset tracks not only sign-ups and auto-reenrollments but also how many enrollees actually paid their first monthly premiums to retain coverage.
analysis_notes: Full article body was paywalled/behind STAT+ login; relied on article headline, lead paragraphs, and contextual links. The data referenced is publicly available at data.cms.gov. No specific biotech companies or drugs discussed.

title: For a Georgia entrepreneur, health coverage has never been affordable
summary: Amy Bielawski, a 61-year-old sole proprietor of an entertainment company in Tucker, Georgia, has largely gone without health insurance for most of her 32-year career running Hare-Brained Productions. After briefly paying for coverage through the ACA marketplace, rising out-of-pocket costs forced her to drop it. The piece is part of STAT's broader 'Out of Pocket, Out of Reach' series examining how uncontrolled healthcare costs are reshaping labor markets and small business economics across America.
analysis_notes: Full article text is paywalled (STAT+ exclusively). Content limited to headline, introductory paragraph describing Amy Bielawski's situation, and boilerplate metadata. The article introduces a case study pattern likely expanded in the full paywalled text with specific insurance cost data.

title: What if this Ebola outbreak can't be stopped?
summary: Authors Ivan Buendia Gayton and Eric D. Perakslis warn that a new Ebola outbreak in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo poses the greatest risk not of spreading to other countries, but of becoming endemic — persisting indefinitely as an ongoing threat. Drawing on their experience during West Africa's 2014-2016 epidemic and the DRC's 2018-2020 outbreaks, they argue that today's conditions lack the extraordinary international cooperation, local adaptations, and luck that helped contain previous epidemics.
analysis_notes: Full article body was behind a STAT+ paywall; analysis based on title, subtitle, author bios, and available excerpt text. The argument centers on endemic persistence risk rather than transnational spread — a distinct framing worth monitoring if DRC outbreak data materializes.

title: This whistleblower took on a health insurance giant and a political machine. She's not stopping there
summary: Chris Deacon, an attorney and health insurance consultant who oversaw New Jersey's health plan for 750,000 state workers, was instrumental in securing a $100 million fraud settlement against Horizon Blue Cross Blue Shield after uncovering allegations that it knowingly overpaid hospitals and doctors and fraudulently won its state contract. Despite being a driving force behind the settlement, Deacon felt frustrated as officials at a press conference largely erased her role and downplayed government culpability in allowing the misconduct to occur for years.
analysis_notes: Full article text is behind a STAT+ paywall. Summary reconstructed from article header, photo caption (identifying Deacon as attorney/consultant behind NJ state workers $100M fraud settlement against Horizon BCBS), opening 3 paragraphs of accessible prose, and topic tags. The $100M Horizon BCBS settlement for alleged fraud against New Jersey's state health insurance contract is the core finding. This article is Part 3 of an 'Out of Pocket, Out of Reach' series examining employer-based health insurance costs.

title: Opinion: Medicine thinks Gen Z is too soft. It’s wrong
summary: The author argues that health care executives should not dismiss young Gen Z doctors as 'too soft' for the medical system. The article counters the stereotype that younger physicians are ill-suited for medicine's demands and advocates for listening to their perspectives instead.

entities.name: University Medical Center of El Paso

analysis_notes: Article body was paywalled; content inferred from headline, subtitle, author bio, and surrounding metadata. Author is a senior vice president of oncology services, writing from personal experience of seeing a young physician in a clinic setting.

title: America's Small Businesses Are Giving Up on Health Insurance
summary: U.S. small businesses are offering health insurance to workers at the lowest rate ever, as the employer-based healthcare system crumbles under rising premiums driven by high hospital, doctor, and prescription drug costs. The percentage of working-age adults covered through jobs has dropped from 67% in 1998 to about 60%, with smaller businesses bearing the brunt of the crisis—many are dropping traditional health benefits entirely out of financial desperation.
analysis_notes: Article content was partially blocked by a paywall (STAT+ subscription required). Retrieved only the top two paragraphs and metadata before encountering repeated 'error' messages. The accessible text establishes the key thesis—small businesses are abandoning health insurance provision at unprecedented rates—but full article analysis, data points, quotes, and deeper findings from the 50+ interviews referenced in the opening were not available. Despite the truncated access, the headline thesis is clear enough to warrant high-priority tagging, as this reflects a structural shift in American employee benefits with broad economic implications.
